what_it_does: This file is a design note that frames the node-identity-across-re-parse problem for annotated documents, compares three anchoring strategies, and recommends combining opaque minted node IDs with text-quote selectors as a re-attachment fallback.
observations: Identifies a collision between path-structured node addresses and the existing flat-opaque board identity law
Lists three anchoring options: opaque node IDs (A), content-hash anchors (B), and text-quote/position anchors (C)
Cites W3C Web Annotation Data Model TextQuoteSelector/TextPositionSelector and URL Text Fragments as prior art
Recommends option A as primary identity plus option C as fallback, with option B as a freshness signal
References existing board concepts: board:// opaque IDs, cas:// hashes, part_of edges, and order fields
standouts: Reframes tree-as-path addressing as a violation of an existing identity discipline rather than a minor fragility
Proposes a hybrid identity model: opaque node IDs for stable identity plus W3C-style quote selectors for re-parse re-attachment
Explicitly owns the silent-orphaning risk if the problem is not decided up front
conventions: Follows noticeboard item YAML frontmatter with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, and history
Uses a markdown comparison table for options
Cites concrete system names and standards (cc_board.py, board://, cas://, W3C Web Annotation Data Model, URL Text Fragments)
Breaks from the common path-based tree-addressing convention by arguing it violates flat-opaque identity
concerns: Does not specify the concrete re-matching algorithm for pairing a re-parsed node to a prior opaque ID
Assumes part_of and order edges already provide everything needed for sequence without showing the schema
Leaves unresolved how identical text collisions are handled when quote selectors overlap
notes: A design argument that the build should use opaque node IDs plus stored text-quote selectors so annotations survive agent edits and re-parses.
questions: What is the exact content-plus-context re-matching algorithm the re-parser will use?
How are opaque node IDs minted, persisted, and garbage-collected across parser changes?
Does the current cc_board implementation already support order edges for sequence among siblings?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to surface the hard node-identity-across-re-parse problem and recommend an anchoring design; the file does exactly that.
apparent_themes: flat opaque identifiers versus path-based addresses
intention_signals: It is trying to prevent the project from silently adopting position-encoded node addresses that would orphan annotations after every agent edit, and to steer the design toward an opaque-ID-plus-quote-selector model.
novelty: It explicitly imports W3C Web Annotation and URL Text Fragment standards into a board identity discussion and frames path-based addressing as a breach of an existing flat-opaque identity law.
contribution: A decision framework of three anchoring options and a concrete recommendation to combine opaque node IDs with text-quote selectors, giving a re-parse-robust way to keep annotations attached after edits.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard design note arguing that node identity across re-parse should use opaque board-style IDs with a part_of edge tree, using W3C text-quote selectors as a fallback re-attachment mechanism instead of position-encoded paths.
intention_for_embedding: To decide up front how annotated document nodes keep stable identity when an agent edits and re-parses the document.
